title: Add podman farm build command
body:
Add podman farm build command that sends out builds to
nodes defined in the farm, builds the images on the farm
nodes, and pulls them back to the local machine to create
a manifest list.

Signed-off-by: Urvashi Mohnani <umohnani@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Add podman farm build command
```

comments.body:
While I finalize the tests, opened the PR to get reviews started on it. Added `no new tests needed` so that the existing tests can run to ensure no regressions are happening.

Docs changes are quite a bit so opened another PR for it here: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20051

Demo of farm build: https://asciinema.org/a/V6WufoZX0Kglvlgq2YfmqZhH7

Adding hold label
/hold

Just happened to see this scroll by, but one thing I wanted to mention here is in the coreos pipeline we basically implement a version of this, see e.g. [this code](https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-pipeline/blob/eb70bf7c73c1e60ae2f354e6df1413685135a2c1/jobs/build-fcos-buildroot.Jenkinsfile#L127-L162).

However, one recent pain point is that we actually want to orchestrate this process inside an OCP cluster, and the container-in-container flow needed [STORAGE_DRIVER=vfs](https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-pipeline/blob/eb70bf7c73c1e60ae2f354e6df1413685135a2c1/jobs/build-fcos-buildroot.Jenkinsfile#L150) which is a well known thing.  However, IMO there's no reason to actually use containers-storage for this, we should basically support using an `oci` directory and that has no special host requirements and should in general be a bit more efficient than deserializing the images only to reserialize them.
I'm slightly surprised to see podman ship this FWIW, I would have said that multi-system orchestration is something we should defer to a higher level tool.

The goal is multi-arch builds.  Assembling multi-arch images is a pain in the butt, we are attempting to make that easier.  Especially on a MAC, where you could have two podman machines running one ARM and one x86.

> I'm slightly surprised to see podman ship this FWIW, I would have said that multi-system orchestration is something we should defer to a higher level tool.

Thanks for checking, @cgwalters !

As Dan mentioned, `farm build` is focusing on simplifying mutli-arch builds only.  It's not meant to orchestrate beyond that.  It boils down to maintaining a list of `podman system connection`s along with the knowledge of the hardware platforms the machines run on.

> It does make sense to have everything in tunnel and use the local socket, 

In `cmd/podman` we force `farm` commands to be executed in "remote" mode.  It's probably easier to move most of the code into `pkg/farm` and pass a "tunnel" runtime around.  Or the stubs of "API" are left empty.

> but given that this PR is currently working, I would like to not block this on making that change. Once this PR is reviewed and merged I can move everything to tunnel, test it out, and open a PR if everything goes well.

I am not a fan of "let's clean up later" (those ghosts hunt us for many years) but I won't block on it as long it's getting done before 4.8.  But I do think it's worth the effort to reduce the redundancy and make it easier to maintain long term.

Added e2e tests!

@vrothberg I created a jira card for making everything "remote" https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RUN-1923.

@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL, tests are mostly green.

the failure happening is because podman's size is going up more than the allowed amount. I am not seeing anything crazy or unnecessary I am pulling in with this feature, but if I am missing something please let me know. If not, is there a way to bypass that requirement?

When I run with just a context directory and no flags, after the final `finished build for ...` message, I get these when I have debugging enabled:
```
DEBU[0008] Looking up image "" in local containers storage 
Error: build: repository name must have at least one component
```
so something's not quite right.  I'd expect to be able to push the final result (a list) by ID, as I can with one gets built using `build --manifest` but without `-t`.
Ephemeral COPR build failed. @containers/packit-build please check.
/bloat_approved
@vrothberg @nalind @flouthoc PTAL
Any man pages?

> Any man pages?

The docs changes are huge so they are in a separate PR here https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20051
@rhatdan @vrothberg  @mheon @nalind can I please get final reviews here
LGTM other then the questions on Platform

> I leave the final head nod to @nalind.
> 
> I am under the impression that podman-remote farm build is not tested in CI which I think is important test coverage.

podman-remote farm and podman farm, should be the same thing, as I understand it.

> podman-remote farm and podman farm, should be the same thing, as I understand it.

Currently they are not.  They're separate code paths.

@Luap99, @umohnani8 opened a separate PR for documentation changes for podman farm build.
https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20051
LGTM

> Okay, I finally understand how this is supposed to work. It's unfortunate that this is not testable in a development environment, but I understand. This is really hard to test. Thank you for all your work on it.

I think this can be tested locally by spawning multiple local services with different root/runroot options.
```
tmpdir1=$(mktemp -d)
tmpdir2=$(mktemp -d)

podman --root "$tmpdir1/root" --runroot "$tmpdir1/runroot" --tmpdir "$tmpdir1/tmp" system service -t0 unix:///$tmpdir1/podman.sock &
podman --root "$tmpdir2/root" --runroot "$tmpdir2/runroot" --tmpdir "$tmpdir2/tmp" system service -t0 unix:///$tmpdir1/podman.sock &

podman system connection add test1 unix:///$tmpdir1/podman.sock
podman system connection add test2 unix:///$tmpdir2/podman.sock

...
```

This should be enough to have more than one socket and test as many connections as you want without depedning on some pre-existing ssh setup.
This is ready, can we get this merged please @rhatdan @nalind

> Waiting for a reply on testing podman-remote as well. It looks like only the local podman is being tested.

My question is still open.  It looks like only the local but  not the remote podman are being tested.

> It looks like only the local but not the remote podman are being tested.

That may not be trivial, I'm not sure off-hand.  Maybe this could be added as a followup PR?

> > It looks like only the local but not the remote podman are being tested.
> 
> That may not be trivial, I'm not sure off-hand. Maybe this could be added as a followup PR?

We don't need to create VMs but use the `podman-remote` binary just as we use `podman`.  It's important to test this code path.

> but use the podman-remote binary just as we use podman

Yes, no new VMs are needed.  It might be a simple case of adding a `matrix` attribute into the task.  What I'm worried about is w/n all the special-case handling is correct since this task runs rootful but ALSO does a rootless setup.  I see no reason to have perfect-CI on the first implementation, @umohnani8 has already put a TON of work into this.  That's why I'm suggesting the rootless case should be dealt with in a followup PR.

The remote code path is already being tested with the builds happening on the farm nodes. I have anyways added one more test with `podman --remote` as well. 
The follow up PR for this feature will be focused on the remote and mac work, so I can definitely look into adding more tests with that if needed. If possible, let's please not block this PR on that.

I had forgotten to update the dependency here after the libimage changes, fixed that up so the bloat should go back down now.

> I see no reason to have perfect-CI on the first implementation, @umohnani8 has already put a TON of work into this. That's why I'm suggesting the rootless case should be dealt with in a followup PR.

I am not asking for perfection but to test the code that is being added; the remote clients are the main target audience of this feature.

I am OK to go ahead as long as we don't advertise the feature before it's being properly tested.  We're close to a release.

I have added tests for the remote case as well and I have manually tested it too. 

With all the libimage and buildah changes, the binary size for podman-remote is still growing by quite a bit. @Luap99 I have used the goda tool you mentioned to try and figure out where the deps are coming from but it is not really giving me much insight, it is also very possible I am not using the tool properly. Some of the farm code is using `containers/storage`, I wonder if that is causing the size to go up by quite a bit.

@umohnani8 Can you try to rebase onto https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20464? This will cause hard compile time failures if libimage is pulled in.

Why do you need to pull in c/storage? This is likey causing the huge bloat. My understanding is that c/storage that should never be used on the client (except type definitions maybe) but I haven't really looked into your code and why you are using it.

@umohnani8 I think the main problem is that you try to recreate a local engine for the local build instead of using the already existing engine interface in the client. I.e it should be possible to pass down `registry.ImageEngine()` to your farm package for the local build instead of trying to create your own interface instance.

On the cli it should look something like this:
```diff
diff --git a/cmd/podman/farm/build.go b/cmd/podman/farm/build.go
index 6acf998fb..7ecb75baf 100644
--- a/cmd/podman/farm/build.go
+++ b/cmd/podman/farm/build.go
@@ -9,8 +9,8 @@ import (
        "github.com/containers/podman/v4/cmd/podman/common"
        "github.com/containers/podman/v4/cmd/podman/registry"
        "github.com/containers/podman/v4/cmd/podman/utils"
+       "github.com/containers/podman/v4/pkg/domain/entities"
        "github.com/containers/podman/v4/pkg/farm"
-       "github.com/containers/storage"
        "github.com/sirupsen/logrus"
        "github.com/spf13/cobra"
 )
@@ -106,17 +106,13 @@ func build(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
                defaultFarm = f
        }
 
-       var storeOptions *storage.StoreOptions
+       var localEngine entities.ImageEngine
        if buildOpts.local {
-               opts, err := storage.DefaultStoreOptionsAutoDetectUID()
-               if err != nil {
-                       return err
-               }
-               storeOptions = &opts
+               localEngine = registry.ImageEngine()
        }
 
        ctx := registry.Context()
-       farm, err := farm.NewFarm(ctx, defaultFarm, storeOptions, buildOpts.local)
+       farm, err := farm.NewFarm(ctx, defaultFarm, localEngine)
        if err != nil {
                return fmt.Errorf("initializing: %w", err)
        }
```

Then you need to make pkg/farm just pass around this interface instead of passing around c/storage options.
The main advantage of this is that it will actually respect the podman cli options such as --root/--runroot and not just work with the defaults.

This is ready to be merge, can we please merge @rhatdan @nalind @vrothberg 

Added remote test and the bloat for both podman and podman-remote is significantly less now.

The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago,umohnani8] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

LGTM
and I concur with @edsantiago .  It's time to merge this and adjust as necessary afterwards.  I'd say mark this as Tech Preview for Fedora v4.8, and then we'll do the same in RHEL 8.10/9.4 and Podman v4.9.
I'm going to let Paul, Valentin, Nalin, or Matt push the shiny red merge button.

/lgtm
/unhold

> I'd say mark this as Tech Preview for Fedora v4.8, and then we'll do the same in RHEL 8.10/9.4 and Podman v4.9.

Please wait with advertising farm build.  We had a meeting earlier this week and decided to merge the PR as is but there will be follow ups changing quite a bit how it's working.  In other words, it's not yet ready for tech preview.

There were some questions about the new `podman farm` already in v4.7, where all it did was edit config files...

```
Usage:
  podman-remote-static farm [command]

Available Commands:
  create      Create a new farm
  list        List all existing farms
  remove      Remove one or more farms
  update      Update an existing farm

```

@vrothberg I'm mostly concerned with putting the Tech Preview on Build Farm for the Podman 4.8/4.9 that lands in RHEL 8.10/9.4 in early February.  Does that work for you?  I'm fine with not marking it Tech Preview for Fedora at the moment while pieces/parts are worked on further.
It is not going to be ready so the commands should be hidden.  We do not document it and will not ship this until podman 5.*
@rhatdan @baude @mheon we have created Features/Epics saying Farm will be in RHEL 8.10/9.4 as Tech Preview, which would be late Jan/early Feb with Podman v4.9.  Do we need to backtrack there?
I think it would be better to wait until 5.0

Respectfully, I'm going to disagree.  I think this is an important feature that distinguishes podman.  I could understand delaying if it's incomplete/not working in some way (is it?).  Otherwise, is there really some important reason to delay getting it into the hands of users?  Could we do a v4.10 for this?

> Could we do a v4.10 for this?

It is now included in `v4.9.0`, but it is mostly undocumented...

"The podman farm suite of commands for multi-architecture builds is now fully enabled and documented."

https://docs.podman.io/en/v4.9.0/markdown/podman-farm.1.html

Especially the need to upgrade all the servers to 4.9 or above.

But also the need to deploy a registry, or configure an existing one.
